summary: Labor corvée register with chiastic poetic structure. 98 cords, 3 colors in perfect mirror: LK-AB-PR-PR-AB-LK (black-subject-action-action-subject-black). Dominated by kama (govern x10) and taka (strike x10). Contains the word waki (communal labor x2) alongside maki (forced labor x3). Ends with an incantation: kama kama kama kama taka kama taka taka. Coverage 96.0%.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  98 cords: 11 INT (sum=55), 75 STRING, 12 EMPTY.
  15 clusters (6 content + 9 empty separators), paired architecture.
  Banco Central de la Reserva del Peru. No provenance recorded.
  Coverage: 96.0% (72/75 STRING cords confirmed).
  3 content colors: LK(29), AB(37), PR(20). Plus SR(3) and ?(9) on empties.

CHIASTIC STRUCTURE
  4 empty cords open. 5 empty cords close. Silence frames the text.
  Between the silences, six chromatic blocks in PERFECT MIRROR:
    LK (black) -> AB (subject) -> PR (action) -> PR (action) -> AB (subject) -> LK (black)
  Black frames subject frames action. The mirror structure of a poem,
  not a ledger. First time this symmetry is observed in the corpus.

VOCABULARY — TWO DOMINANT WORDS
  kama (to govern/create): x10 — the word of power
  taka (to strike/stake): x10 — the word of action
  pama (formula): x6 — the ritual formula of convocation
  maki (hand/labor): x3 — the corvée itself (mit'a)
  paka (hidden/secret): x3 — undeclared work?
  kaka (maternal uncle): x3 — the guarantor
  waki (communal labor): x2 — voluntary reciprocal work
  paki (to break): x2 — breaking stone, construction
  wama (falcon): x2 — the overseer?
  tata (father): x2, mama (mother): x2, wapa (administrator): x2
  Single: chaka (bridge), sika (climb), taki (ritual chant),
  wata (year), kaki (dried corn/Pleiades), pata (terrain), tama (herd)

SECTIONS

  I. THE BLACK OPENING — CONVOCATION (cords 5-21, LK)
    PASS-pa, sika, kama, DIR-ta — passive, to climb, to govern, toward.
    Someone climbs toward the governor. The CALL to mit'a.
    maki — hand/labor. The central word of corvée.
    pama x3 — the formula, three times. The ritual words of convocation.
    waki — communal labor. The reciprocal frame.
    paki, maki, chaka, taka — to break, to work, the bridge, to strike.
    The four gestures of corvée: break stone, work hands, build bridge,
    strike ground.
    Numbers: 10, 7. Ten days? Seven workers?

  II. THE SUBJECTS — CONTRIBUTORS (cords 22-43, AB)
    tata, wapaka, taka, kaka, papa, pata, tama, paka-ACC.
    The LIST OF CONTRIBUTORS: father, administrator, uncle, father,
    terrain, herd, secret. Each named by kinship and property —
    the basis of labor tax.
    taki-ACC (cord 40) — the ritual chant[object]. The chant is
    obligatory, codified. Part of the corvée, not decoration.
    Number: 4.

  III. THE WORK (cords 44-63, PR)
    maki, wata, paka x2, paki, waki-DIR, pataki, kamaki.
    The labor itself: work, year, secret, break, communal labor.
    pama, wama, PASS-pama — formula, falcon, formula[passive].
    The falcon (wama) enters as overseer — watching from above.
    takama, kama, DIR-wama — strike-toward, govern, toward-falcon.
    Production directed to the overseer.
    Number: 5.

  IV. THE SUBJECTS RETURN (cords 64-76, AB)
    Mirror of section II. kama, kaka, taka, mama, pama, kama x2.
    The mother (mama) appears — women inscribed for textile/food work.
    Numbers: 5, 3, 4. Per-person or per-day counts.

  V. THE BLACK CLOSING — INCANTATION (cords 77-88, LK)
    mama — mother. kaki — dried corn / Pleiades. The astronomical
    marker that triggers the labor season.
    Then: kama, kama, kama, kama — govern, govern, govern, govern.
    Four times. Like a drum beat.
    taka — strike. One blow between the governance.
    kama — govern again. Five kama total, one taka inserted.
    Numbers: 5, 4.
    taka, taka — strike, strike. The final two words.
    This is a POWER INCANTATION — the rhythm of the hammer on stone,
    of the foot on earth, of the corvée chant.

THE WAKI/MAKI DISTINCTION
  waki (communal labor, x2) vs maki (forced labor, x3).
  Both coexist on the same khipu: the governor convokes for mit'a
  (forced labor), but the formula uses waki (voluntary reciprocity).
  The obligation is dressed in reciprocity. The state corvée is
  framed as communal work.

COMPARISON
  UR051 is unique in the corpus. No other khipu has:
    - Perfect chiastic color structure (LK-AB-PR-PR-AB-LK)
    - A governance incantation (kama x5 + taka x3)
    - The waki/maki duality
    - Silence frames (4 empty + 5 empty)

NARRATIVE — THE CORVÉE CHANT

  Four cords of silence. Then the convocation begins.

  Someone climbs toward the governor. The formula is spoken — three
  times, because formulas must be repeated. The work is named:
  maki, the hand. The communal labor is named too: waki, the
  shared effort. The governor calls it corvée; the formula calls
  it reciprocity.

  The four gestures: break the stone, work the hands, build the
  bridge, strike the earth. Ten and seven — days or workers.

  The color changes from black to brown. The subjects are listed:
  the father, the uncle, the administrator, the terrain, the herd.
  Each contributor named by blood and property. The chant is noted
  too — the workers must sing. It is written at the accusative:
  the chant is an object of the corvée, not a choice.

  Red-brown now: the work itself. The hand, the year, the secret.
  Something is hidden in the labor — undeclared days, uncounted
  work. The stone breaks. The falcon watches from above — the
  overseer, the one who sees everything.

  Brown again: the subjects return. The mirror reflects. The uncle,
  the mother — she too is inscribed, for spinning and cooking.
  The formula. The governor.

  Then black returns, and the khipu becomes something else.

  The mother. The dried corn. The Pleiades — the star cluster
  whose rising marks the harvest season, the beginning of labor.
  And then:

  Govern. Govern. Govern. Govern. Strike. Govern. Five and four.
  Strike. Strike.

  This is not a ledger. This is a rhythm. The hammer on the stone,
  the foot on the packed earth, the chant that the workers sang
  as they built the bridge and broke the rock. The khipu does not
  just record the corvée — it carries its beat.

  Five cords of silence. The hammer stops.

CAVEAT
  This is the strangest khipu in the series. The chiastic structure
  and the kama-kama-kama-kama-taka incantation are unusual — it
  resembles a ritual text more than a true labor register. The
  classifier assigned it labor_tribute because of maki (labor x3)
  and paki (to break x2), but the tone is odd.
  It may be a CORVÉE FORMULARY — the standard text recited when
  convening workers, not the register of days worked. Something
  like a decree vs a timesheet.
references: Sivan 2026, ALBA Project — Locke Word Splitting, chiastic structure analysis
Mit'a (corvée) and waki (communal labor): Murra 1980, Economic Organization of the Inca State
Kaki/Pleiades as agricultural calendar marker: Urton 1981, At the Crossroads of the Earth and Sky
Taki (ritual chant) as obligatory labor component: Randall 1993, Qoyllur Rit'i
Chiastic literary structure in Andean oral tradition: Harrison 1989, Signs, Songs, and Memory in the Andes
